coredata.dc:title: Peroxidase-like copper-doped carbon-dots embedded in hydrogels for stimuli-responsive bacterial biofilm elimination and wound healing

coredata.dc:description:
Bacterial biofilms and their microenvironment are significant challenges that must be faced in the design of antibacterial drugs. Microenvironment-responsive mimetic peroxidases (POD) have been demonstrated to be an efficient solution to eliminating bacterial biofilms. However, they inevitably require additional H2O2 and/or acid due to the poor permeabilities towards biofilms. Herein, we report POD-like copper-doped carbon dots (named CuCD1) synthesized through a facile microwave-assisted carbonization manner. The characteristics of ultrasmall size (< 5 nm) and positive charge enabled it to possess good penetrability toward bacterial biofilm. As expected, CuCD1 showed great damage to bacteria due to the generation of hydroxyl radicals (•OH), which originated from the catalytic decomposition of endogenous H2O2 under a weak acid bacterial biofilm microenvironment. This highly increased oxidative stress resulted in the alteration of cell membrane permeability, subsequent cell death, and the final eradication of bacterial biofilm and the exposed bacteria. Moreover, to verify the practicality in vivo, CuCD1 was introduced to a routine hydrogel that was crosslinked by carboxymethyl chitosan (CMCS) and oxidized dextran (ODEX). In comparison with the control groups, the composite hydrogel, i.e., CuCD1-CMCS-ODEX revealed better antibacterial performance and thus accelerated wound healing and collagen disposition. This work would open opportunities to design CDs-based biofilm microenvironment-responsive antibacterial nanoagents.
Statement of significance (1) Ultrasmall size, positively charged, peroxidase (POD)-like CuCD1 were designed and harvested by a facile microwave-assisted carbonization method. (2) CuCD1 revealed a competitive in vitro antibacterial performance, good penetrability, and microenvironment-responsive clearing capacity towards bacterial biofilm. (3) By composing with CMCS-ODEX hydrogel, the composite hydrogel could continuously eliminate bacteria, promote wound healing, as well as collagen disposition. (4) This work would provide a new strategy in the design of CDs-based biofilm microenvironment-responsive antibacterial nano-agents.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterizations of the CuCDs. (A-D) TEM images and size distributions (inset) of the CuCDs, respectively. FTIR spectra (E), ICP-AES (F) (n=3), and zeta potential measurements of the CuCDs (G). (H) The UV–vis absorption spectra of TMB (1 mM) in the absence and presence of different CuCDs. (I) The fluorescence emission spectra of the TA probe in the absence and presence of different CuCDs.

In vitro antibiofilm performance. (A) 3D LCSM images of the E. coli biofilm (stained by SYTO-9, green channel) treated by CuCD1 (blue channel) Scale bar: 50 µm. (B) SEM observations of S. aureus and E. coli biofilms with different treatments, scale bar: 50 µm. (C) 3D LCSM images of S. aureus and E. coli biofilms stained with SYTO-9/PI, scale bar: 100 µm. (D) Pictures of the biofilms stained by crystal violet (CV). (E) The quantification of biofilm elimination was calculated by measuring the absorbance of CV at a wavelength of 590 nm.

(A) Agar plate-coating digital photographs of bacterial colonies of S. aureus and E. coli treated with different concentrations of CuCD1, respectively. The corresponding bacterial counts of S. aureus (B) and E. coli (C) after treatment with different concentrations of CuCD1. (D) Representative LCSM images for a live/dead bacterial viability assay of S. aureus and E. coli with CD1 and CuCD1 treatments. Scale bar: 25 µm. (E) DCFH fluorescence images of S. aureus and E. coli with different treatments. Scale bar: 25 µm. (F) SEM images of S. aureus (Scale bar: 3 µm) and E. coli (Scale bar: 2 µm) with different treatments. (G) Gel electrophoresis of DNA extracted from E. coli with different treatments. Significance analysis was carried out by one-way ANOVA and t-test. * p < 0.05, ** p < 0.01, *** p < 0.001, n = 3.

Preparation and characterizations of the CuCD1-CMCS-ODEX hydrogel and the CMCS-ODEX hydrogel. (A) Schematic illustration of the synthesis process of hydrogel. (B) Photographs of the conversion from CMCS and ODEX solutions to hydrogel. Storage modulus (G') and loss modulus (G'') of CuCD1-CMCS-ODEX hydrogel and CMCS-ODEX hydrogel at (C) frequency sweep measurement and (D) oscillatory strain amplitude sweep measurement. (E) G' and G'' of the CuCD1-CMCS-ODEX hydrogel and the CMCS-ODEX hydrogel at alternate strain between low strain (1 %) and considerable strain (400 %) with 1.0 rad/s of fixed frequency. SEM images of the CMCS-ODEX hydrogel (F) and the CuCD1-CMCS-ODEX hydrogel (G), respectively. Scale bar: 500 µm. (H) SEM elemental mapping of the CuCD1-CMCS-ODEX composite hydrogel. Scale bar: 500 µm.

In vivo antibacterial application. (A) Illustration for the establishment of the mice wound infection model and treatment regime. (B) Photographs of the wounds in different groups. (C) The wound healing trace in different groups. (D) The wound contraction rate in different groups (n = 3). (E) The bacterial elimination efficiencies in the wounds with different treatments (n = 3). (F) Body weight changes of S. aureus-infected mice after different treatments during the seven days. HE (G) staining and Masson staining (H) of the wound tissues. Scale bar: 200 µm (magnification scale bar is 50 µm).

(A) Preparation and screening of POD-like CuCDs candidates. (B) Mechanism illustration of CuCD1-based microenvironment responsive bacterial biofilm disruption. (C) Preparation of CuCD1-CMCS-ODEX composite hydrogel and its application for wound healing.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Bacterial biofilms and their microenvironment are significant challenges that must be faced in the design of antibacterial drugs. Microenvironment-responsive mimetic peroxidases (POD) have been demonstrated to be an efficient solution to eliminating bacterial biofilms. However, they inevitably require additional H2O2 and/or acid due to the poor permeabilities towards biofilms. Herein, we report POD-like copper-doped carbon dots (named CuCD1) synthesized through a facile microwave-assisted carbonization manner. The characteristics of ultrasmall size (< 5 nm) and positive charge enabled it to possess good penetrability toward bacterial biofilm. As expected, CuCD1 showed great damage to bacteria due to the generation of hydroxyl radicals (•OH), which originated from the catalytic decomposition of endogenous H2O2 under a weak acid bacterial biofilm microenvironment. This highly increased oxidative stress resulted in the alteration of cell membrane permeability, subsequent cell death, and the final eradication of bacterial biofilm and the exposed bacteria. Moreover, to verify the practicality in vivo, CuCD1 was introduced to a routine hydrogel that was crosslinked by carboxymethyl chitosan (CMCS) and oxidized dextran (ODEX). In comparison with the control groups, the composite hydrogel, i.e., CuCD1-CMCS-ODEX revealed better antibacterial performance and thus accelerated wound healing and collagen disposition. This work would open opportunities to design CDs-based biofilm microenvironment-responsive antibacterial nanoagents.

(1) Ultrasmall size, positively charged, peroxidase (POD)-like CuCD1 were designed and harvested by a facile microwave-assisted carbonization method. (2) CuCD1 revealed a competitive in vitro antibacterial performance, good penetrability, and microenvironment-responsive clearing capacity towards bacterial biofilm. (3) By composing with CMCS-ODEX hydrogel, the composite hydrogel could continuously eliminate bacteria, promote wound healing, as well as collagen disposition. (4) This work would provide a new strategy in the design of CDs-based biofilm microenvironment-responsive antibacterial nano-agents.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infections are a major global public health concern [1,2], with around 80 % of these infections associated with biofilms [3,4]. Biofilms are complex microbial communities enclosed in a protective layer of extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) [5,6]. EPS, composed of polysaccharides, proteins, lipids, and extracellular DNA, comprise 90 % of the biofilm mass [7–9]. The biofilm microenvironment has distinct chemical and physical properties, such as hypoxia [3,10–12], high hydrogen peroxide levels (50–100 μM) [13,14], low pH values (4.5–6.5) [15–17], and increased hydrolase expression [18,19], which create great barriers against antibiotics and the immune system [20,21]. Current antibacterial agents, such as antibiotics, cationic polymers, and antimicrobial peptides, are mostly effective against planktonic bacteria [22]. Therefore, designing new agents capable of penetrating and disrupting biofilms holds great promise for treating bacterial biofilm infections [21,23–25].

Currently, stimulus-responsive nanoparticles have been demonstrated to be a promising solution for combating bacterial biofilm infections [3]. With microenvironment-responsive catalytic activity, nanozymes have been extensively researched for their broad-spectrum antibacterial properties and low toxicity [26]. Among them, mimetic peroxidase nanomaterials can convert low toxic H2O2 into highly destructive •OH, thus attracting broad attention for their antibacterial properties. [13,27–29]. To date, several POD-like nanomaterials, for instance, metallic oxide, carbon materials, noble metal nanoparticles, and their composite materials have shown obvious antibacterial activities [30–36]. Despite the demonstrated good antibacterial activity both in vitro and in infectious wounds, most of them inevitably require exogenous H2O2 to bring a synergistic antibacterial effect. In addition, their large particle sizes and negatively charged surface greatly limited their performance in biofilm permeability and elimination [37].

Recently, an emerging and promising antibacterial agent, transition metal doped CDs (M-CDs) has attracted increasing attention due to its outstanding merits of ultrasmall size, abundant surface groups, water-soluble, and good biocompatibility [38]. The doping of transition metals (e.g., Fe, Co, Cu, Ce) endowed CDs with additional physicochemical characteristics, especially a POD-like property, and thus they revealed competitive antibacterial activity as expected [39–42]. Although these studies have demonstrated the antibacterial effects of M-CDs, it is essential to note that the applications of these nano-agents still require exogenous H2O2 and/or acidification pre-treatments [41]. Besides, most of them still suffer from relatively poor biofilm permeability and finite biofilm elimination, mainly attributed to their negatively charged surface [40]. Therefore, it remains a great challenge to rationally design surface-controllable M-CDs antibacterial nano-agents with good biofilm permeability and enhanced biofilm elimination capacity in the absence of exogenous H2O2 or faintly acid.

Encouraged by the above findings, we herein report a microwave-assisted carbonization method to synthesize CuCDs with adjustable surface potential (Scheme 1 A). Among them, CuCD1 was selected as a candidate agent due to its unique physical-chemical properties, such as strong POD catalytic activity, positive surface charge, ultrasmall size, good water solubility, and monodispersity. These properties are beneficial for facilitating easy passage through biofilm water channels and rapid penetration into the interior of biofilms (Scheme 1B). By encapsulating CuCD1 into CMCS-ODEX, the composite hydrogel was utilized to enhance antibacterial activity, wound healing, and collagen deposition (Scheme 1C), demonstrating its great potential for in vivo antibacterial application.

CuCl2, citric acid (CA), lysine (Lys), and 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Terephthalic acid (TA), 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein (DCFH-DA) were obtained from Aladdin Chemistry Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). Dulbecco's modified Eagle's medium (DMEM) was obtained from Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus, AB 91-093) and Escherichia coli (E. coli, AB 93-154) were purchased from China Center for Type Culture Collection. The bacterial genomic DNA extraction kit and the CCK-8 kit were purchased from Solarbio Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Deionized (DI) water was used throughout the study.

The sizes and morphologies of CuCDs and CDs were characterized by a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEM-2100F, Japan). The content of doped Cu in the CuCDs was measured using ARCOS inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometer (ICP-AES). Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was obtained by a FTIR spectrometer (IS50, Thermo Fisher, USA). The elemental compositions of CuCDs were measured with an X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) (AXIS SUPRA, UK). Zeta potentials were obtained by Zetasizer Nano Z (Malvern, UK). The UV–vis spectra of the samples were obtained by Cary 300 (Agilent, USA) spectrophotometer. A scanning electron microscope (SEM, Hitachi, Regulus 8230) was used to observe bacterial morphology. Laser confocal scanning microscopy (LCSM) images were obtained by TCS SP8 (Leica, Germany). The rheological properties of the hydrogel were determined using the HR-2 rheometer (WATERS, USA) at a temperature of 25 °C. Dynamic strain scanning measurements (γ = 0.1–1000 %) were conducted to obtain the critical strain point of the hydrogel. G' (storage modulus) and G″ (loss modulus) of the hydrogels were measured through dynamic frequency scan experiments at a strain of 2 % and frequencies ranging from 1 to 100 rad/s. Meanwhile, amplitude oscillatory strains at alternate high (500 %) and low (1 %) were performed at 1 Hz frequency for 100 s in each step to further investigate the self-healing property. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Hitachi, Regulus 8230) was used to observe the morphology and pore size of the CuCD1-CMCS-ODEX hydrogel and the CMCS-ODEX hydrogel.

CuCDs were prepared using a facile microwave-assisted carbonization method [39,41]. Briefly, citric acid and lysine (0.17 g) in molar ratios (0:1, 0.25:1, 1:1, and 3:1) were dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water. The mixture was sonicated for 5 minutes, after which 0.17 g of copper chloride was added. The resulting aqueous solution was heated in a microwave oven at 700 W for 5 minutes to obtain a caramelized black-brown solid. After cooling to room temperature, the samples were dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water, filtered through a 0.22 µm microporous membrane to remove insoluble materials, and dialyzed against water for 48 hours. The final products (i.e., CuCD1, CuCD2, CuCD3, and CuCD4) were then obtained by freeze-drying and stored at room temperature. The synthesis of CDs without Cu doping was identical to that of CuCDs.

To obtain oxidized dextran (ODEX), 2 g of dextran was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water (100 mg/mL), and 1.32 g of sodium periodate (dissolved in 12 mL of water) was added. The reaction was conducted at room temperature in the dark for 4 hours. Then, 1 mL of ethylene glycol was added to the mixture to stop the oxidation reaction. The mixture was then dialyzed in a dialysis membrane (MW=3500) for three days and freeze-dried to obtain ODEX. Afterward, 300 mg CMCS and 1 mg of CuCD1 were dissolved in 10 mL H2O, and then an identical volume of ODEX (w/w. 2 %) was added. Under ultrasonic shaking, the composite hydrogel i.e., CuCD1-CMCS-ODEX was obtained.

ce:section.ce:section-title: POD-like activity and kinetic assay of CuCDs
ce:section.ce:para: The POD-like activity of CuCDs was assessed using a classical TMB method [43–45]. Briefly, in acetate buffer (0.2 M, pH 4.0), 20 µL of 10 mM TMB, 20 µL of 10 mM H2O2, and 20 µL of CuCDs (500 µg/mL) were added. The UV–vis absorption spectrum (652 nm) was measured after the reaction for 30 min at room temperature to study the catalytic activity. Then, 20 µL of 10 mM TMB in acetate buffer (0.2 M, pH 4.0), 20 µL CuCDs (500 µg/mL), and different concentrations of H2O2 from 0 to 300 mM were added for kinetic assays. The reaction was conducted at room temperature for 60 minutes, and the UV-visible absorption spectrum at 652 nm was recorded for each sample.

Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal plot was used to describe the enzymatic reaction rate of CuCDs. The Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) and the maximum initial velocity (Vmax) were calculated as follows:

Where V represents the initial reaction velocity, Km represents the Michaelis constant, [S] is the concentration of substrate, and Vmax is the maximal reaction velocity [46–49].

The generation of •OH was detected and verified using terephthalic acid (TA) fluorescence probe [47,48]. In brief, TA, H2O2, and CuCDs were mixed in acetate buffer (pH 4.0, 20 mM). The final concentrations of TA, CuCDs, and H2O2 were 0.5 mM, 10 µg/mL, and 10 mM, respectively. After incubation at 37°C for 3 hours, the samples were subjected to fluorescence measurements (λex=315 nm, λem=430 nm).

S. aureus and E. coli were cultured in tryptic soy broth (TSB) and Luria-Bertani (LB) media, respectively. A single colony was transferred from the plate into 3 mL of medium and incubated overnight at 37 °C on a constant-temperature shaker at 180 rpm to activate the colony. To reach a logarithmic growth phase, 100 µL of the bacterial solution was taken and diluted into 3 mL of fresh medium. The mixture was then shaken at 37 °C on a thermostatic shaker set at 180 rpm for 6 hours.

To evaluate the penetration capacity of CuCD1 into biofilms. 100 µL of E. coli bacterial solution (1×107 cfu/mL) was incubated in 900 µL of LB medium in 48-well plates at 37°C without shaking for 48 hours to allow the formation of mature biofilms. The medium was replaced every 24 hours. After two days, the upper layer of the medium was discarded and the biofilms were washed three times with PBS to remove planktonic bacteria. 1 mL of CuCD1 (100 μg/mL) was added to E. coli biofilms, and SYTO-9 was used to stain the biofilms for 20 minutes in the dark. The penetration of CuCD1 into E. coli biofilms were observed using a laser scanning confocal microscope (LSCM) after 2 hours.

100 µL of bacterial solution (1×107 cfu/mL) was incubated in 900 µL of TSB medium or LB medium in 48-well plates at 37°C without shaking for 48 hours to allow the formation of mature biofilms. The medium was replaced every 24 hours. After 48 hours, the upper layer of the medium was discarded and the biofilms were washed three times with PBS to remove planktonic bacteria.

Then, 1 mL samples containing PBS, CuCD1, and CD1 (control group, without Cu doping) were incubated for 24 hours at 37°C without shaking. After 24 hours of incubation, the supernatant was discarded, and the bacteria were washed three times with physiological saline to remove any planktonic bacteria, then fixed with 2.5 % glutaraldehyde for 4 hours at 37 °C. The resultant samples were dehydrated in a series of alcohol solutions (10 %, 30 %, 50 %, 70 %, 90 %, 100 %). Finally, the dried samples were coated with gold (Au) before SEM observation.

Based on the above mature biofilm culture, 1 mL samples of PBS, CuCD1, and CD1 were incubated for 24 hours at 37°C without shaking, respectively. After 24 hours, the supernatant was discarded, and the bacteria were washed three times with physiological saline to remove any planktonic bacteria. Subsequently, the treated biofilms were incubated with SYTO-9/PI for 15 minutes and washed again with physiological saline. All z-sections were collected at 20-μm intervals using a confocal laser scanning microscope.

Based on the above mature biofilm culture, 1 mL samples of PBS, CuCD1, and CD1 were incubated for 24 hours at 37°C without shaking. After washing with PBS, the biofilms at the bottom of the well plate were fixed with 95 % ethanol for 15 minutes and stained with 200 µL of 0.5 % crystal violet solution for 5 minutes. Finally, the crystal violet was removed, and the plate was washed three times with sterile water. To quantify the biofilm formation, 33 % acetic acid was added, and the absorbance of the crystal violet was measured at 590 nm.

The bacterial solution with an OD600 value of 0.1 was further diluted 103 times with PBS buffer. Then, PBS and different concentrations of CuCD1 (10–200 µg/mL) were mixed with the bacterial solution at 37°C and 180 rpm for 8 hours. After incubation, 100 µL of the bacterial solutions were taken and spread evenly on agar plates, incubated overnight at 37 °C, and photographed for colony counting.

The morphologies of bacteria before and after being treated with CuCD1/CD1 were analyzed using SEM. In the logarithmic growth phase, S. aureus and E. coli were inoculated on sterile silicon wafers and incubated overnight at 37°C, 180 rpm. The bacteria were then treated with 50 µg/mL CuCD1 and 50 µg/mL CD1, respectively, for 12 hours. All wafers were rinsed three times with PBS, fixed with 2.5 % glutaraldehyde at 37°C for 4 hours, and then washed thoroughly with PBS more than three times to remove the residual glutaraldehyde. The immobilized bacteria were then dehydrated progressively with 10 %, 30 %, 50 %, 70 %, 80 %, 90 %, and 100 % ethanol solutions. The dried wafers were gold-coated for subsequent SEM observations.

In the logarithmic growth stage, S. aureus and E. coli were washed three times with saline and then co-incubated with the samples at 37°C, 180 rpm for 6 hours. The samples were then incubated with a SYTO-9/PI premix in the dark for 30 minutes. Finally, the stained bacterial cells were observed using a LSCM.

The DCFH-DA kit was used to measure reactive oxygen species (ROS) levels in bacterial cells. S. aureus and E. coli in the logarithmic growth phase were washed three times with PBS, and then incubated with CuCD1 at 37°C, 180 rpm for 3 hours. After washing three times with PBS, the treated samples and 1 mL (100 µM) of DCFH-DA were incubated for 30 minutes at 37°C, protected from light. Intracellular ROS levels were observed using an LSCM.

In vitro, the cytotoxicity of CuCD1 at diverse concentrations (0, 5, 10, 25, 50, 75, 100, 150, and 200 µg/mL) was carefully evaluated using the cell counting kit-8 (CCK-8) experiments. MCF-7 cells were used as the model cells for the cytotoxicity assays. The MCF-7 cells were seeded into 96-well plates (2×105 cells per well) and cultured in an incubator containing 5 % CO2 at 37 °C for 24 hours. The resulting cells were then treated with CuCD1 for an additional 24 hours. Afterward, the cells were washed three times with sterile PBS, and a fresh culture medium (100 µL) containing CCK-8 reagent was added into each well and incubated for 1 hour. The absorbance at 495 nm in each well was measured using a microplate reader.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo antibacterial and wound healing
ce:section.ce:para: All animal experiments in this study were conducted in compliance with the experimental animal ethics review of Wenzhou Medical University (Approval number: wydw2023—0383) and the Chinese Guide for the Care and Use of Laboratory Animals. To establish a bacterial infection model of skin wounds, 7-week-old Balb/c mice were anesthetized with 0.5 % sodium pentobarbital. The mice were randomly divided into the control group (PBS), the CMCS-ODEX hydrogel group, and the CuCD1-CMCS-ODEX hydrogel group. The upper back region of the mice was depilated using a cream, and a round skin section of approximately 8 mm in diameter was excised after sterilizing with 75 % ethanol. Immediately after the surgery, a 20 µL suspension of fresh S. aureus containing 2×107 cfu was injected into the surgical area. On the first postoperative day, the CMCS-ODEX hydrogel and the CuCD1-CMCS-ODEX hydrogel were injected into the surgical area for wound anti-infection treatment (PBS was injected as the control). Throughout the treatment period, the body weight of the mice was measured using an electronic balance. Additionally, the wound sites were photographed every two days using a digital camera, and wound sizes were analyzed using Image J software. To confirm the in vivo antimicrobial efficacy of the CMCS-ODEX group and the CuCD1-CMCS-ODEX group, a routine plate counting method was employed.

The mice were euthanized on the 4th and 10th day in compliance with ethical guidelines, and samples of back skin tissue were collected and preserved in a 4 % paraformaldehyde solution. The infection sites were then examined using different staining techniques for pathology analysis. Additionally, on the final day of the experiment, vital organs, including the heart, liver, spleen, lung, and kidney, were harvested from each mouse and processed using classical H&E and Masson staining assays. All tissue samples were embedded in paraffin and sectioned.

All experiments were repeated at least three times and presented as mean ± SD. Statistical significance was calculated using unpaired Student's two-sided t-test, and statistically significant differences are shown with *p < 0.05, **p < 0.01, ***p < 0.001, and ns: no significant.

The structure, morphology, and composition of the obtained CuCDs were characterized in detail. As seen in the HRTEM images (Fig.1 A-D), all the harvested CuCDs reveal spherical shapes, small particle sizes (< 5 nm), and narrow size distributions. In Fig. 1E, the typical absorption peaks at 3100–3300 cm-1, 1718 cm-1, 1315–1480 cm-1, and 1050–1213 cm-1 can be assigned to N-H, C=O, C-N and C-O respectively. These hydrophilic groups contribute to the good water-solubility of the CuCDs. Besides, it is easy to find that as the amount of lysine increases in CuCDs synthesis, the symmetric stretching vibrations of C-N (1315–1480 cm-1) and the amino group (3100–3300 cm-1) become more pronounced. Moreover, the presence of a weak peak at approximately 1010 cm−1, attributed to Cu-N groups, suggests the successful formation of the copper coordination compound. Subsequent XPS results (Fig. S1) have verified and supported these FTIR analyses. To determine the oxidation state of Cu, high-resolution XPS analysis (Fig. S2) of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and Cu 2p of CuCD1 (as an example) was performed. Detailed explanations of the C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s spectra can be found in our previous report on CuCDs [41]. As to Cu 2p (Fig. S2D), the peaks at 950.3 eV and 930.9 eV correspond to the spin-orbit splitting of Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 originating from Cu2+, respectively [51]. The peak at 929.1 eV is attributed to the Cu 2p3/2 features of Cu+. ICP-AES results (Fig. 1F) illustrate that CuCD1 has the highest Cu doping. Notably, the Cu doping level is positively correlated with the amount of surface amino groups, mainly attributed to the strong coordination between Cu and N [39,50]. Zeta potential measurements (Fig. 1G) indicate that only CuCD1 was positively charged (+21.72 mV).

Next, the photophysical properties of the CuCDs were measured, and they were found to be similar to those of most citric acid or amino acid-derived CDs. They exhibited nearly identical UV–vis absorption spectrum and fluorescence spectrum. As shown in Fig. S3, a prominent band (250 nm-400 nm) with a peak centered at 275 nm indicates the presence of π−π* of the sample, which is probably assigned to the excited state of the surface defect [52–55]. They have shown a typical blue fluorescence, with optimal excitation and emission around 340 nm and 420 nm, respectively. This typical emission feature is ascribed to surface state/defect-induced emission [55–57]. Furthermore, the photostability of CuCD1 was also inspected. As shown in Fig. S4, CuCD1 reveals good photostability under continuous ultraviolet light irradiation, relatively stable emission at pH values from 6 to 10, and good tolerance to high ionic strengths up to 250 mM of NaCl.

Transition metal ions possess easily lost electrons and unoccupied orbitals in their outer shells. When metallic ions are doped into CDs, this enhances the potential for variations in charge density and charge transition forms between the metal ions and the CDs, which may lead to the emergence of new physicochemical properties, such as enhanced catalytic properties [58–60].. To assess the catalytic activity of CuCDs, a classic TMB method was employed. As shown in Fig. 1H, under the optimized pH value of 4 (Fig. S5), CuCD1 resulted in the most prominent blue color change and a significant absorption band (550 nm to 750 nm) with a peak centered around 652 nm, attributed to the typical absorption of oxidized TMB [61,62]. However, the CDs without Cu doping did not produce any color change (Fig. S6A), implying they had no peroxidase-like catalytic activity. This oxidation process of TMB is associated with the generation of •OH via an H2O2 oxidation reaction, catalyzed by CuCDs mimicking peroxidase under weakly acidic conditions [41]. This was confirmed using a specific •OH fluorescent probe i.e., terephthalic acid (TA). As seen from Fig. 1I, only CuCD1 caused significant fluorescence enhancement, implying that CuCD1 exhibited stronger catalytic activity in comparison with other CuCDs. In addition, CuCD1 also revealed better POD-like catalytic activity than other metal-doped CDs, such as Fe/Ce/Co-CDs (Fig. S6B), likely due to the higher redox potential of Cu2+. The enzyme parameters Km and Vmax of the CuCDs were measured, as shown in Fig. S7. The catalytic stability of CuCD1 was assessed over different storage periods, revealing good storage stability even up to two years under conventional maintenance conditions (dark, dry, and room temperature), as shown in Fig. S8. Considering the amount of Cu doping and surface charge, we speculated that CuCD1 holds great potential for catalysis and antibacterial applications. Thus, we focused on further research and application of CuCD1 in subsequent studies.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Biofilm penetration and removal by CuCD1
ce:section.ce:para: Previous studies have confirmed that ultrasmall-sized, positively charged antibacterial nanoagents possess the ability to penetrate the interior of bacterial biofilms, and subsequently disrupt the structures of biofilm [22,63–65]. Building upon these findings, CuCD1 is expected to exhibit significant biofilm penetration and eradication capabilities. To investigate this, E. coli biofilm was employed as a model system and treated with CuCD1 to assess its penetration and accumulation within mature biofilms. As shown in Fig. 2A , LCSM analysis revealed that E. coli cells in the biofilm stained with SYTO-9 showed bright green fluorescence, accompanied by blue fluorescence signals from CuCD1 distributed throughout the interior of the biofilm. This spatial distribution pattern demonstrates the effective penetration capability of CuCD1 into the biofilm matrix. This phenomenon might be attributed to the strong electrostatic interaction between CuCD1 and the negatively charged EPS layer, along with the ultrasmall size of CuCD1, which facilitates rapid diffusion through the aqueous channels within the E. coli biofilm [4,66].

To further investigate the biofilm eradication capability of CuCD1, we extended the incubation period to 12 hours. SEM analysis revealed distinct morphological differences between treatment groups (Fig. 2B). The control group confirmed that the bacteria could form dense biofilms on the coverslip surfaces, with bacteria cells clumped together and embedded within the EPS matrix. In contrast, CuCD1 treatment effectively disrupted the dense structure of biofilms, significantly decreasing the number of bacterial colonies compared to the CD1 group. The 3D laser confocal microscopy further demonstrated that the control and CD1-treated samples showed pronounced green fluorescence (Fig. 2C and Fig. S9A and B), which exhibited densely packed living cells within biofilms. In contrast, the CuCD1 group predominantly exhibited red fluorescence with a minimal amount of green fluorescence, suggesting effective bacterial inactivation. Subsequently, crystal violet staining was employed to detect biofilm quantitatively. The noticeable color transition from purple to pale blue, demonstrated the significant antibiofilm activity of CuCD1, with inhibition rates of 73 % and 46 % against S. aureus and E. coli, respectively (Fig. 2D and E). These above findings demonstrated that the proposed CuCD1 exhibits remarkable biofilm penetration and eradication capabilities, making it a promising candidate for combating biofilm-associated infections.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibacterial properties and mechanism of CuCD1
ce:section.ce:para: To systematically evaluate the antimicrobial efficacy of CuCD1, we conducted comparative studies using CD1 (denoted as the control, without Cu doping) against Gram-positive (S. aureus) and Gram-negative (E. coli). Through quantitative plate counting assays conducted after 12 hours incubation of S. aureus and E. coli with varying concentrations of CuCD1, we observed a concentration-dependent reduction in bacterial viability (Fig. 3A ). Notably, CuCD1 exhibited potent antibacterial activity even at remarkably low concentrations (as low as 5 µg/mL) (Fig. 3B and C). In addition, CuCD1 demonstrated superior antibacterial performance against S. aureus relative to E. coli, which can be attributed to the structural complexity and compositional differences in the cell wall architecture of E. coli [67]. Furthermore, a detailed comparison of antibacterial performance between CuCD1 and various nanomaterials (M-CDs and nanozymes) in Table S1 demonstrated the superior antimicrobial profile of CuCD1. Specifically, CuCD1 exhibited competitively and revealed comprehensive advantages: (1) low minimum inhibitory concentration, (2) enhanced biofilm penetration and eradication capabilities, and (3) self-sufficient antimicrobial action without requiring external stimuli such as H2O2 supplementation, pH adjustment, or light activation.

To investigate the antibacterial mechanism of CuCD1, the initial assessment of antibacterial efficacy was conducted using LCSM. S. aureus and E. coli were separately treated with CuCD1 and CD1, followed by dual staining with SYTO-9 and PI for viability assessment prior to LCSM imaging. As shown in Fig. 3D, both control and CD1 treatment groups exhibited intense green fluorescence (indicating live bacteria) and negligible red fluorescence (indicating dead bacteria). By contrast, CuCD1-treated samples exhibited only red fluorescence, demonstrating near-complete bacterial eradication. SEM was employed to observe the morphology and membrane integrity of the bacteria (Fig. 3F). Control samples maintained characteristic intact morphology for both S. aureus (spherical) and E. coli (rod-shaped). CD1 treatment induced partial membrane wrinkling and disruption, while CuCD1 exposure resulted in extensive structural damage, with complete loss of native bacterial morphology. In addition, ROS generation was quantitatively assessed using the specific fluorescent probe 2′,7′-dichlorofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) (Fig. 3E). As seen therein, the CuCD1 group exhibited obvious green fluorescence, indicating a significant generation of ROS in bacteria. This observation implies that the POD-liked activity of CuCD1 catalyzes the conversion of endogenous H2O2 to •OH under the faintly acid, high H2O2 level microenvironment of bacterial biofilm. The resulting oxidative stress contributes to membrane disruption and cellular damage.

The compromised integrity of bacterial cell membranes enables efficient penetration of positively charged CuCD1. As shown in Fig. S10, after being exposed to CuCD1, the bacteria reveal obvious cyan fluorescence, indicating bacteria cells have good uptake capacity towards CuCD1. CuCD1 interacts with bacterial DNA through electrostatic interactions, thereby inhibiting DNA replication and enhancing its antimicrobial activity. To further investigate this mechanism, we conducted agarose gel electrophoresis analysis of E. coli genomic DNA following treatment with CD1 and CuCD1 (Fig. 3G). The control group exhibited a single, intact DNA band above 10,000 bp, which indicated that the DNA molecular weight was homogeneous and the DNA structure was relatively intact (lanes 2). However, the E. coli genomic DNA treated with CD1 displayed fragmented DNA patterns (lanes 3), suggesting partial DNA degradation. More importantly, the major band in the CuCD1-treated group (lanes 4) showed a significant reduction compared to the other groups, providing direct evidence of extensive genomic damage. These findings indicate that CuCD1 induces fragmentation of bacterial genomic DNA into smaller segments, thereby disrupting essential biological functions, while concurrently causing structural damage to the cell membrane integrity.

Based on the comprehensive analysis of the experimental results, the antibacterial mechanism of CuCD1 is tightly associated with its specific surface chemical properties. The positively charged CuCD1 facilitates efficient bacterial penetration through electrostatic interactions with negatively charged bacterial surfaces. The POD-like activity of CuCD1 triggers a burst of ROS within bacteria calls, leading to the structural destruction of bacterial walls and membranes. This process also causes cytoplasmic leakage and severe DNA damage. These synergistic effects collectively contribute to the favorable antibacterial activity observed in CuCD1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the composite hydrogel
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the therapeutic potential of CuCD1 in anti-infection and tissue repair, we developed a routine CMCS-ODEX hydrogel system using carboxymethyl chitosan (CMCS) and oxidized dextran (ODEX) as the polymeric matrix. The objective of this study was to engineer and characterize the multifunctional CuCD1-CMCS-ODEX hydrogel composite with optimal wound dressing properties encompassing essential characteristics such as appropriate mechanical strength, injectability, self-healing capability, and sustained antimicrobial activity.

First, the physical and chemical properties of both CuCD1-CMCS-ODEX hydrogel and CMCS-ODEX hydrogel were systematically investigated. As depicted in Fig. 4A , the hydrogel formation mechanism involves a Schiff base reaction between the primary amine groups of CMCS and the aldehyde groups of Dex-CHO. Fig. 4B demonstrates the hydrogel formation process, where combining CMCS and ODEX solutions swiftly generates a crosslinked network at room temperature. Strain sweep assay revealed that CMCS-ODEX hydrogel and CuCD1-CMCS-ODEX hydrogel maintain structural integrity up to approximately 100 % strain (Fig. 4C), beyond which the hydrogel network undergoes collapse. Frequency sweep measurements showed that the storage modulus (G') values of the CuCD1-CMCS-ODEX hydrogel and CMCS-ODEX hydrogel are consistently higher than the loss modulus (G'') across the entire frequency range. This indicates the exceptional mechanical properties of the CMCS-ODEX hydrogel and the CuCD1-CMCS-ODEX hydrogel (Fig. 4D). Hydrogel prepared through dynamic Schiff base reactions typically exhibits favorable self-healing properties. Therefore, the self-healing features of the CuCD1-CMCS-ODEX hydrogel and CMCS-ODEX hydrogel were carried out using a dynamic step-strain test (Fig.4E). When subjected to high strain conditions (≈ 400 %), exceeding the critical point by four-fold, G'' surpassing G', indicating that the network structure of the CuCD1-CMCS-ODEX hydrogel and CMCS-ODEX hydrogel collapsed. However, upon returning to low strain conditions (1 %), the G' value rapidly increased, and the hydrogel recovered to its original state. This reversible behavior persisted through three consecutive test cycles, confirming the autonomous self-healing capability of the hydrogel network. Morphological characterization through SEM revealed that CMCS-ODEX hydrogel (Fig. 4F) and CuCD1-CMCS-ODEX hydrogel (Fig. 4G) exhibit porous and interconnected structures with pore sizes of tens of microns, which endow the dressings with good breathability. EDS elemental mapping (Fig. 4H) indicates the distribution of C, O, N, and Cu in the composite hydrogel. The presence and uniform dispersion of Cu elements specifically validate the successful incorporation and even distribution of CuCD1 within the hydrogel network.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo antibacterial application of CuCD1
ce:section.ce:para: Building upon the exceptional in vitro antibacterial performance of CuCD1, we sought to explore its therapeutic potential in wound healing applications through incorporation into the composite hydrogel system. Before in vivo evaluation, we conducted a study on the cytotoxicity of CuCD1 (Fig. S11). The cell viability assay revealed that CuCD1 maintained approximately 80 % cell survival rate even at a concentration of 200 µg/mL, indicating favorable cytocompatibility within the therapeutic concentration range.

The experimental design for evaluating wound healing efficacy is schematically diagramed in Fig. 5A , detailing the infection and treatment protocol using a skin wound model with bacterial infection. After 24 hours of sustaining the bacterial infection, mice were randomly divided into three groups, including control, CMCS-ODEX hydrogel, and composite hydrogel. Wound dressings were systematically replaced every two days, with photographic documentation and wound area measurements throughout the study. As demonstrated in Fig. 5B and C, the wound sizes in each group exhibited a gradual decrease over time. Notably, the composite hydrogel group demonstrated superior wound closure, achieving near-complete re-epithelialization by day 7, with a relative wound residue rate of 6.70 ± 2.03 % and the presence of new skin tissue. The wound residue rates of the control and the CMCS-ODEX hydrogel treatment groups were 22.05 ± 3.23 % and 18.50 ± 4.1 %, respectively (Fig. 5D). The change in the number of bacteria on the wound surface was studied via the plate-coating method to evaluate the in vivo antibacterial activity. Compared with the control group, the number of bacteria on the wound surface sharply reduced after treatment with the composite hydrogel (Fig. 5E). Agar plate digital photographs of bacterial counts on wound surfaces after different days of treatment are shown in Fig. S12. The marked reduction in wound surface bacterial load following composite hydrogel treatment suggests its potential to mitigate infection and promote tissue regeneration. Furthermore, throughout the experimental period, all treatment groups maintained stable body weights without significant fluctuations (Fig. 5F), indicating that none of these treatments affected the individual growth of the mice. These results suggest that the composite hydrogel effectively provides sustained antibacterial activity at the wound site while supporting tissue regeneration.

Wound healing typically occurs chronologically, including hemostasis, inflammation, proliferation, and tissue remodeling [68]. To verify the impact of the CMCS-ODEX hydrogel and the composite hydrogel on the processes mentioned above, we evaluated the process of wound healing based on histological changes. On day seven after treatment, we collected the wound tissues from mice in all groups and performed hematoxylin-eosin (HE) and Masson staining analysis, respectively. As shown in Fig. 5G and H, several inflammatory cells were observed in the skin tissues of the control group. However, after being treated by the CMCS-ODEX hydrogel, the number of inflammatory cells was reduced to some degree. In contrast, in the composite hydrogel group, there was almost no infiltration of inflammatory cells, and a noticeable presence of newly arranged epithelial cells and connective tissue was observed. Additionally, the observation revealed the emergence of new structures resembling hair follicles. Furthermore, to investigate the biosafety of CuCD1, HE staining on organs (liver, heart, spleen, lung, and kidney) was performed, and the results implied that no observable organ damage and inflammatory lesion for CuCD1 treatment (Fig. S13). The above results have verified that CuCD1 reveals low cytotoxicity, good biosafety, as well as great potential for in vivo anti-infection and wound healing.

In summary, we successfully developed and characterized ultrasmall size, positively charged, water-soluble CuCD1 with POD-like activity. The unique physicochemical properties of CuCD1 endow it with exceptional biofilm penetration capability and potent antimicrobial efficacy. CuCD1 efficiently catalyzed endogenous H2O2 into hydroxyl radicals within acid and the high H2O2 level microenvironment in bacterial biofilm, leading to the destruction and clearance of biofilms. This catalytic process generates substantial reactive ROS, which destroy bacterial walls and membranes, and cause serious cytoplasmic leakage and DNA damage, resulting in good in vitro antimicrobial performance. Furthermore, the therapeutic potential of CuCD1 was further demonstrated through its incorporation into a CMCS-ODEX composite hydrogel system. In the bacterial infection wound model, the CuCD1-CMCS-ODEX composite hydrogel revealed a more competitive antibacterial activity, which could accelerate wound healing and promote collagen disposition. Besides, to broaden its versatility, antibacterial performance research of CuCD1 on drug-resistant bacteria and fungus is ongoing.
Data will be made available on request.
